mnemonics: Alpha-bet Soup: The α (alpha) level is the significance level you set before your test, like choosing your soup before you taste it. The p-value is the actual 'spice' found in the data. Reject the null hypothesis (H₀) only if the p-value is less spicy than your chosen α.
keyPoints: The significance level (α) is the probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true (a Type I error).
Common pre-set α levels are 0.05 (5%), 0.01 (1%), and 0.001 (0.1%), representing the threshold for 'statistical significance'.
The p-value is calculated from the sample data. If the p-value is less than or equal to the chosen α level, the result is statistically significant, and the null hypothesis is rejected.
commonMistakes: Believing a lower p-value means a more important or larger effect size. A p-value only indicates the strength of evidence against the null hypothesis, not the magnitude of the result.
Confusing the significance level (α) with the p-value. α is a pre-determined threshold for making a decision, while the p-value is a computed result from the data.
